input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a Living Lab case study, a real-world infrastructure test environment comprising a building or facility equipped with IoT sensors, actuators, and integrated cyber-physical systems. This Living Lab serves as a representative AECO domain asset, enabling the integration of static models with real-time operational data for comprehensive simulation, analysis, and optimization of building lifecycle processes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Digital Twins provide simulation, analysis, and optimization services, delivering real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities for data-driven infrastructure management in the AECO domain.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical assets and digital twins through continuous data exchange for live monitoring and immediate insights in infrastructure management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Model-driven framework with modular architecture integrating Eclipse Hono and Ditto for IoT data management and Unreal Engine for high-fidelity visualization, operating as a central hub for component orchestration and resource coordination.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: BIM-derived geometric and semantic models for building components (sensors, actuators, controllers), transformed via Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles into structured DT artifacts; real-time data exchange synchronizes physical infrastructure with digital counterparts; modular architecture integrates custom analytics services for operational insights.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts through bidirectional data flows facilitated by the DT Framework.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Facility Managers interact with the deployed digital twin for real-time monitoring and control, with bidirectional data exchange enabling control signals to be transmitted to physical building systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Nonfunctional requirements address interoperability across heterogeneous systems, though specific technical network infrastructure details such as communication protocols, topology, or security measures are not explicitly detailed in the provided documentation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The framework employs a modular architecture with standardized extension points for scalable deployment, supporting runtime configuration management to enable adjustments without full redeployment, and addresses scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems as part of nonfunctional requirements. Interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses four key stages: (1) initial device modeling within BIM environments by Building Control Engineers, where sensors, actuators, and controllers are defined to establish a semantic foundation; (2) Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation of enriched BIM models into structured, deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention; (3) real-time data synchronization between physical infrastructure and the digital twin via continuous data exchange; and (4) evolution through modular extension points enabling DT Engineers to integrate custom analytics services, with Facility Managers utilizing interactive visualization for operational monitoring and control. Lifecycle governance is role-based, ensuring seamless transitions between stages through standardized workflows.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles are applied to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. The modular architecture's standardized extension points facilitate the integration of custom services and analytics, enabling adaptive evolution of the Digital Twin based on operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real-time data exchange enables continuous synchronization between physical assets and their digital twins, with interactive visualization providing immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. Custom analytics services integrated via standardized extension points generate actionable insights for operational decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework's modular architecture includes standardized extension points to enable interoperable integration of custom services and analytics.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the 'Living Lab case study' as the physical system and correctly describes its purpose in the AECO domain, such as simulation, analysis, and optimization. It aligns well with the source's definition of the physical asset being twinned. Supported by quotes: 'We demonstrate the practical application of our approach in a Living Lab case study' and 'creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization'.

The extraction accurately reflects the services described in the Introduction and Abstract. It correctly captures the services provided: 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization' and 'real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities' for 'data-driven infrastructure management in the AECO domain'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly lists specific tools and technologies used. The text mentions Building Information Modeling (BIM), Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine as key enablers. Supported by quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization' and 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the document's description of the temporal aspects as 'real-time' and 'live'. The abstract states the framework enables 'real-time monitoring' and 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'. Additionally, the text mentions 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins'. The extraction aligns perfectly with these mentions, though it lacks specific numerical latency data not present in the text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The text mentions 'modular architecture' and 'service-oriented DT platforms', but it does not describe multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs. decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins. Therefore, the information is absent.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed summary of the system architecture found in the text. It correctly identifies the 'model-driven framework' and its 'modular architecture'. It accurately lists the integrated components: 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' for data (described in the text as for 'real-time data exchange') and 'Unreal Engine' for visualization (described as 'rich, interactive visualization'). It also captures the system's role as a 'central hub' for coordination, mirroring the text's description of the 'DT Framework acts as a central hub'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions 'interoperability' and 'fragmented data sources', and integrates 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' (which serve as internal connectivity middleware), it does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins. The information is not present.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly accurate and detailed, directly reflecting the paper's core contributions. It correctly identifies BIM as the source for geometric and semantic models, MDE principles for transformation, Eclipse Hono/Ditto for real-time data exchange, and the modular architecture for services. Quotes: 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment, grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

The provided source text discusses a framework for deploying Digital Twins and mentions 'actuators' and 'lighting systems' only in the context of modeling capabilities or research context (TwinLight/BOREALIS), rather than describing specific physical acting components of the system (types, ranges, limitations). The text focuses on the *methodology* of the framework rather than the hardware specifications. Quote: 'device modeling capabilities... to define sensors, actuators, and controllers'. Since the text ends before the detailed case study (Section V), specific component details are absent.

Similar to the acting components, the text mentions sensors in the context of the framework's requirements ('sensor configurations') and modeling capabilities, but does not describe the specific sensing infrastructure (sensor types, sampling frequencies, spatial distribution) within the provided snippets. The text emphasizes the *modeling* of these components, not the components themselves. Quote: 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'.

The text mentions the intent to evaluate the Digital Twin using the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' and briefly references 'validating the model', but it does not describe model fidelity requirements, specific verification methods, uncertainty sources, or quality assurance processes in detail within the provided snippets. The mention is too brief to constitute a description of the characteristic as defined. Quote: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

The extraction provides an excellent, detailed summary of the deployment architecture, accurately synthesizing information about modularity, extension points, runtime configuration, and scalability. It is strongly grounded in the text and captures the nonfunctional requirements regarding deployment effectively. Supported by: 'Runtime configuration management further enhances adaptability by enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment' and 'modular architecture with defined extension points'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
